title: Give expressive openings one dominant visual plane
principle: When an expressive marketing page needs motion, let one dominant visual plane establish identity while secondary sections remain quieter and task-led.
rationale: The reviewed gallery separates hero, section, component, and background patterns; that taxonomy is useful only when the product hierarchy chooses which layer deserves attention.
observations: The public catalog groups complete pages, sections, components, and animated backgrounds separately.
Premium and copyable examples coexist, so availability is not evidence of product fit or reuse permission.
applies_when: An occasional-use marketing, portfolio, or launch page has a verified reason for an expressive first viewport.
exceptions: Dense operational interfaces and high-frequency tasks should not inherit gallery-style focal motion.
Unknown or paid prompt terms block prompt, screenshot, and expression copying.
implementation: Choose the primary reading action before selecting a visual reference.
Assign one focal motion role and reduce amplitude, contrast, and repetition elsewhere.
Rewrite any referenced pattern into the project's type, color, content, and interaction system.
verification: The first viewport still has one clear headline, support sentence, and action with motion disabled.
No copied prompt, screenshot, metric, testimonial, or branded expression appears in the implementation.
accessibility: Keep ordinary reading content available without animation and define an equivalent reduced-motion outcome.
responsive: Recompose the focal plane for narrow and short viewports instead of shrinking a desktop spectacle.
performance: Budget animated media against LCP, memory, battery, and input responsiveness.
browser_notes: Treat advanced background and scroll effects as progressive enhancement.

title: Use component catalogs for discovery before adaptation
principle: Use an animated component catalog to name and compare candidate behaviors, then select or adapt only after item-level license, semantic, state, responsive, dependency, and motion review.
rationale: A large copy-oriented catalog improves findability, but the catalog surface does not prove that any specific component fits the product or meets production requirements.
observations: The public surface advertises React, Framer, copy, and MCP access.
The visible responsive skeleton changes column count across viewport bands.
applies_when: A project lacks a component for a specific, already-defined interaction need.
exceptions: Prefer a native element or an existing project primitive when it already satisfies the behavior.
implementation: Write the component contract and state matrix before catalog search.
Inspect the exact item and license before adapting code.
Retest the result inside realistic project content rather than trusting the demo.
verification: Keyboard, names, focus, errors, touch, reduced motion, and narrow-content behavior are observed in the integrated component.
No dependency or code is installed solely because an MCP or catalog result suggested it.
accessibility: A catalog demo is not accessibility evidence.
responsive: Test the integrated component between named breakpoints and with long content.
performance: Account for animation and framework dependencies before adoption.
browser_notes: Confirm APIs and fallbacks against maintained primary documentation.

title: Keep animated typography subordinate to reading
principle: Use animated text as one bounded identity or feedback moment only when the unchanged text remains readable, selectable, semantic, and immediately available in reduced motion.
rationale: A broad pure-CSS effect inventory shows many possible treatments, but effect variety is not a reason to animate repeated reading content.
observations: The reviewed surface groups 66 self-contained CSS effects.
The source states that its effects use color tokens and respect reduced motion.
applies_when: A display word or short status can carry a deliberate visual beat without delaying comprehension.
exceptions: Do not animate long text, instructions, errors, prices, deadlines, or frequently reread labels.
implementation: Keep the real text node as the accessible source of truth.
Use decorative pseudo-elements without duplicating accessible text.
Remove looping animation once it has conveyed its purpose.
verification: The content is understandable with CSS disabled and with prefers-reduced-motion enabled.
Text selection, zoom, forced colors, and translation expansion remain usable.
accessibility: Never encode meaning in visual distortion, color change, or motion alone.
responsive: Check line wrapping before adding per-letter wrappers or absolute layers.
performance: Prefer transform and opacity where appropriate; measure paint-heavy filters and gradients.
browser_notes: Provide a plain-text fallback when an effect depends on unsupported CSS.

title: Treat interruption as part of the motion contract
principle: Choose spring-like motion only after defining state ownership, interruption, reversal, cancellation, and final semantic agreement; a spring curve alone does not make an interaction continuous.
rationale: The reviewed library correctly emphasizes current velocity and interruption, but some public snippets simplify semantics, content measurement, or pointer behavior and therefore remain hypotheses rather than production components.
observations: The source contrasts fixed-duration easing with motion that continues from current state.
Its catalog includes direct manipulation, feedback, layout, and decorative examples with varied implementation completeness.
applies_when: State transitions or direct manipulation benefit from continuity across rapid input.
exceptions: Frequent tasks may need immediate or low-amplitude feedback instead of overshoot.
Decorative loops do not become purposeful because they use spring terminology.
implementation: Model visual state from the latest valid application state.
Define pointer capture, cancellation, bounds, and keyboard alternatives for gestures.
Tune constants on representative hardware and content.
verification: Interrupt and reverse the interaction during every phase.
Confirm DOM state, focus, accessible value, and rendered position agree after cancellation and rapid repeat input.
accessibility: Provide equivalent discrete controls and a reduced-motion state change.
responsive: Retest gesture bounds after resize, rotation, and dynamic content changes.
performance: Measure layout and paint cost; a CSS snippet is not proof of no layout work.
browser_notes: Use pointer events and feature fallbacks consistent with the supported browser baseline.

title: Design terminal interfaces for terminal constraints
principle: Treat a terminal UI as its own keyboard-first platform with explicit focus, layout, scrolling, color, resize, and text-rendering contracts rather than styling a web page to resemble a terminal.
rationale: The official surface exposes terminal-native components, focus handling, flexible layout, syntax rendering, and framework bindings as actual capabilities, not decorative chrome.
observations: The official homepage lists Text, Box, Input, Select, ScrollBox, Code, Diff, focus management, and timeline animation.
The project provides TypeScript bindings over a native core.
applies_when: The requested product actually runs in a terminal environment.
exceptions: A developer-tool website should not inherit terminal visuals unless the product narrative and interaction require them.
implementation: Choose terminal-native components and define focus and key bindings before color styling.
Plan resize, narrow columns, scrolling, selection, and fallback color behavior.
verification: Run the TUI in supported terminals and dimensions.
Verify keyboard completion, focus visibility, copy/select behavior, and readable output without full color support.
accessibility: Document terminal and assistive-technology limits honestly; do not infer screen-reader support from component names.
responsive: Treat terminal resize and narrow columns as first-class reflow conditions.
performance: Measure native binding startup and rendering for the target workload.
browser_notes: Not applicable to web delivery; keep platform guidance separate.

title: Name the conventional interaction before inventing it
principle: Translate a visual description into the conventional component and platform term before implementation so semantics, native options, expected states, and maintained primitives can be evaluated precisely.
rationale: A visual dictionary is useful for moving from vague appearance language to search terms, but terminology must still be checked against authoritative platform and accessibility guidance.
observations: The reviewed dictionary maps plain-language descriptions to conventional names and representative API or semantic cues.
Entries distinguish visually similar elements such as headers and navigation, dividers and separators, or progress and indeterminate waiting.
applies_when: A request describes an element by appearance or behavior but does not name the interaction pattern.
exceptions: Do not force an existing custom interaction into an ill-fitting conventional label.
implementation: Record the likely conventional name and native semantic base.
Check keyboard, focus, state, and naming requirements in primary documentation before coding.
verification: The implemented element matches the named interaction contract, not merely its appearance.
User-facing labels remain familiar and descriptive.
accessibility: Treat dictionary cues as discovery leads, not normative ARIA guidance.
responsive: Verify the named pattern's responsive transformation and touch behavior.
performance: Prefer the simplest native or maintained implementation that fulfills the contract.
browser_notes: Confirm platform API names against current primary documentation.

title: Separate visual-tool inspiration from asset rights
principle: Use visual tooling to explore color, gradient, duotone, or logo directions only after defining the product role, then verify the exact asset and tool terms before public or commercial use.
rationale: A collection of creative tools can improve the exploration vocabulary without granting blanket rights to generated marks, source imagery, or branded output.
observations: The reviewed surface separates logo, pigment, gradient, and duotone tools.
The terms identify a commercial operator but do not provide a blanket license for every generated asset.
applies_when: A visual system needs a specific asset transformation or palette exploration.
exceptions: Do not use generated logos or third-party imagery without trademark and source-rights review.
implementation: Write the asset's product purpose and required rights before generation.
Keep source, license, attribution, and transformation evidence with the asset.
verification: The final asset has recorded rights and attribution status.
Contrast, crop, responsive sizing, and non-visual alternatives are verified.
accessibility: Decorative transformations must not obscure text or remove required alternatives.
responsive: Generate or export appropriate aspect ratios and sizes rather than cropping blindly.
browser_notes: Provide fallbacks for unsupported image or color features.

title: Make 3D product presentation a progressive layer
principle: Use interactive 3D product presentation only when it explains material, shape, configuration, or motion better than static media, with a complete static and non-pointer fallback.
rationale: The reviewed product surface centers model upload, materials, lighting, animation, and export, but its public shell does not prove accessibility, device support, performance, or output integrity.
observations: The public description frames 3D as a configurable product-visualization workflow rather than generic decoration.
The server-rendered shell uses a full-screen loading treatment before the interactive application.
applies_when: A product's spatial or material properties are central to the user's decision.
exceptions: Prefer static images, diagrams, or video when 3D adds cost without new understanding.
implementation: Define a static poster and text description before WebGL integration.
Expose discrete camera, material, and animation controls when direct manipulation is not accessible.
verification: Test loading, failure, low-power, keyboard, reduced-motion, and no-WebGL paths.
Measure memory, frame pacing, interaction latency, and export accuracy on target devices.
accessibility: Provide non-pointer controls and equivalent descriptive information.
responsive: Reframe controls and model bounds for narrow, touch, and short viewports.
performance: Budget model size, texture memory, shaders, and render loop cost.
browser_notes: Feature-detect WebGL capabilities and preserve a static fallback.

title: Decompose references into decisions before remixing
principle: Capture reference structure, hierarchy, motion, and implementation constraints as separate observations, then produce variants from the product brief instead of rerolling or copying the reference expression.
rationale: The reviewed workflow repository treats prompts, captures, and skills as versioned assets; the transferable value is the explicit decomposition and iteration contract, not the supplied demos or presets.
observations: The repository separates capture workflows, implementation skills, references, and runnable demos.
Its published workflow emphasizes clear constraints, hierarchy, and bounded iteration.
applies_when: A visual reference, screen recording, or existing page materially informs a new direction.
exceptions: Skip reference capture when the task is a tiny local fix with no art-direction decision.
implementation: Record observed facts separately from inferred behavior.
Generate materially different candidates from the same product constraints.
Keep a ledger of applied and rejected reference evidence.
verification: The selected direction can explain each borrowed principle without reproducing source expression.
Candidates differ in composition and system, not only color.
accessibility: Reference appearance never overrides semantic, focus, reflow, or reduced-motion requirements.
responsive: Infer responsive behavior explicitly and verify it in rendered output.
performance: Reject reference effects that exceed the project's measured budget.
browser_notes: Validate recreated behavior in the project's browser baseline.
